book.kubejs.minecraft_start_guide.name: Ascension Primer
book.kubejs.minecraft_start_guide.toolti: The survival and progression manual for Ascension of Ages.
book.kubejs.minecraft_start_guide.primer: The first rules of survival and the logic behind the opening era.
The world opens with pressure, not power.

The Dark Ages are about stability. You begin weak, thirsty, and exposed to weather. Do not sprint toward a ruin just because you can see one. Ask simpler questions first: can you stay warm, can you carry clean water, and can you survive the coming night or season shift?

Gather loose materials, knap your first stone tools through Overgeared, secure wood and shelter, light a fire, and start planning for thirst and temperature early. The opening quests are not filler. They teach the survival loop the rest of the pack expects you to respect.
A good first day is repeatable, not flashy.

Your core loop: gather Flint, knock loose Rocks, shape stone tool heads, add sticks, cut wood, place a Campfire, craft a Terracotta Bowl, and start working toward purified water. A chest, a fire, and a roof beat a long corpse-run after dark.

Progress is not the first interesting item you find. Progress is a camp that refills food, water, and healing faster than the world drains them. When you can leave, return, and improve your camp in one trip, the pack opens up.
book.kubejs.minecraft_start_guide.age0_p: Dark Ages Survival
The Dark Ages: hardship, slow food, real exposure, and the first careful exploration.
Primitive Tools
Wood and simple tools are meant to feel earned.
Crude Hands

The early pack blocks the normal rush on purpose. Right-click a stone block with Flint to knock loose Rocks, shape tool heads in the knapping grid, and assemble stone tools with sticks. Then move into wood, camp infrastructure, and proper shelter. The pack wants your first fire and first safe camp to matter.
Recipe Guidance

Treat the early recipes as a ladder: Flint gets you Rocks, knapped heads get you stone tools, stone tools get you wood, and wood gets you camp infrastructure. Copper and the Overgeared forging chain come next in Entering the Iron Era. If a quest names a tool step, finish it before you try to skip ahead.
Water, Weather, and Wounds
Thirst Was Taken and Cold Sweat tie water, weather, and temperature into one problem.
Purify What You Carry

Thirst Was Taken makes hydration part of the loop. Craft a Terracotta Bowl early, then heat-treat filled water containers in a Furnace or Smoker to raise purity. A Waterskin helps on the road. Carry more than one drink before long trips, especially in hot biomes or summer.
Treat The Right Problem

Food does not fix cold or thirst on its own. Shelter, a Boiler, insulating armor from Goat Fur or Chameleon Molt, and route discipline matter. The Thermometer and Calendar from Ecliptic Seasons exist because biome and season can be as dangerous as a mob if you ignore them.
Food and Shelter
Early food is slower by design.
Manual Farming Is Intentional

Dark Ages food should feel manual, local, and survival-focused. Wheat, potatoes, fishing, and simple cooked meals matter more than abundance. Nutritional Balance means one lucky meat stack is not a forever plan, and that is deliberate.
Simple Meal Guidance

Use the quests to push three habits: keep backup road food, cook at least one better meal at home, and start storing ingredients instead of carrying everything. A roof, a fire, and a pantry turn hunger from panic into planning.
Safe Exploration
Exploration is a loop of leaving and returning, not a straight line away from spawn.
Pack Before Distance

Carry torches, backup food, purified water, and a Sleeping Bag before you chase structures. Leather armor and better weapons come later. A good trip ends back at base with more resources and better information than you started with.
Read Structures

Not every structure is meant to be rushed blind. Learn to scout, retreat, and mark routes. Early rewards lean on compasses, maps, and structure clues. Waystones arrive much later in Renaissance, so plan return trips on foot first.
book.kubejs.minecraft_start_guide.age1_f: Medieval Establishment
Medieval Times: durable tools, dungeon prep, and the first machine footholds that grow out of a stable camp.
Ironbound
Iron is the point where survival stops feeling desperate and starts feeling established.
What Iron Means

Medieval establishment begins when the Dark Ages survival loop is stable enough to support real equipment. Iron comes through Overgeared forging, not grid crafting. With secure water, leather armor, and a base worth returning to, you can branch into dungeons, treasure, and low-tier industry without living in triage mode.
Do Not Skip The Middle
This is still not the factory age. You are meant to become competent, not untouchable. Stronger treasure appears here, but complete machines and next-age power spikes stay gated on purpose.
Dungeon Readiness
A real delve kit is a branch milestone, not flavor.
Scout First

A scout pack means torches, food, purified water, armor you can trust, and an exit plan. The first successful ruin should be a chosen fight, not a reckless full-clear. Quest rewards like compasses and structure clues make exploration more directed here.
What Unlocks Here

Medieval delving is where relic hunts, larger ruins, and stronger treasure branches start to matter. The reward is not just loot. It is access to better targets, harder locks, and routes worth repeating.
First Works
Your first machine jobs should still feel physical and small.
Recipe Guidance

Medieval machine work starts with hand power and simple Create parts. Craft shafts and cogwheels, then a Hand Crank and Millstone so one real job becomes easier. Immersive Engineering and larger industry stay later. The First Mill chapter is the foothold, not the finish line.
A Mill, Not A Factory

The point of this branch is to give you a foothold. One useful machine, one rotation setup, one job that saves labor. Medieval establishment should teach what machines are for without turning the whole pack into a solved automation puzzle.
book.kubejs.minecraft_start_guide.treasu: Locked loot is part of progression, not random decoration.
Not every lock means the same danger or reward.

Clavis locks scale with chest value and punish mistakes. Failed picks cost health and loot quality; a failed pick does not open the chest. Higher-value chests and later treasure bands expect better prep, not just better luck. The richest endgame chests are framework only for now.

Early Dark Ages treasure must not skip metal progression. No raw ore jackpots, no ore blocks, no meaningful ingot piles, and no easy machine starts. Medieval treasure can be stronger, but it still must not hand out bulk metals, complete machines, or next-age power spikes.
Treasure is unlocked through progression, not only luck.

Basic locked chests can appear early. Harder survival and exploration milestones are planned to unlock richer treasure bands through progression stages. Quest rewards already lean on maps, structure clues, compasses, and tokens so hunting stays intentional.

The pack is trying to make exploration and treasure feel earned instead of passive. A structure clue means something changed in your progression. A tougher lock means the game expects better prep, not just better luck.
book.kubejs.minecraft_start_guide.food_f: Food starts manual, then opens into relief later.
Diet quality is part of the survival loop.

Nutritional Balance keeps food variety relevant alongside Thirst Was Taken and Cold Sweat. Farmer's Delight and simple crops solve the early age slowly on purpose. The quests should teach you to keep several ingredients moving instead of one infinite stack of safe food.

Keep one cheap travel food, one better home meal, and at least one crop plan you can sustain. Cook because it improves the loop, not because the quests want random busywork. The slow early kitchen is intentional pack design.
The pack does plan to reduce farming tedium later.

Medieval Times is where food and farming relief starts to open. Create hand power, the First Mill, and later Create-integrated farming, Slice and Dice, and Cooking for Blockheads answer the manual Dark Ages kitchen. That relief is earned, not given at spawn.

The Dark Ages are supposed to make food feel scarce, local, and earned. If the opening era feels slower than a kitchen automation pack, that is intentional. Later automation rewards you for getting through that pressure, not for skipping it.
book.kubejs.minecraft_start_guide.age2_t: Developer/testing-only scaffold; normal progression should ignore it.
Developer-only scaffold, not a player route.

This section is developer/testing-only. Normal progression should ignore it and follow the live questbook age chapters. It is kept here so pack builders can document temporary scaffold routes without presenting them as an available player path.

If a test world is granted this scaffold externally, use it to probe content breadth, recipe flow, boss readiness, and automation relief. Do not treat it as balance or as a route players can unlock in normal play.
book.kubejs.minecraft_start_guide.future: Frameworks for the later age tree.
The later ages exist as structure now, not finished balance.

After Medieval comes Renaissance, then Industrial Revolution, Gilded Age, Atomic, Otherworldly, and Ascension. Each group adds systems, dimensions, bosses, and harder treasure bands. The long arc is authored chapter by chapter.

Later groups already exist as structure even when individual chapters are unfinished. Quests, stages, treasure rules, and guide content are being shaped now so the pack can scale without a full rewrite.
